Numerical Linear Algebra

The Fundamental Theorem of Linear Algebra, Orthonormal Bases, and Orthogonal Projectors

The Fundamental Theorem of Linear Algebra, Orthonormal Bases, and Orthogonal Projectors

The previous chapter established that the four fundamental subspaces of A∈Rm×nA \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}A∈Rm×n pair up into two orthogonal complements: R(A)⊥=N(AT)R(A)^\perp = N(A^{\mathsf T})R(A)⊥=N(AT) and R(AT)⊥=N(A)R(A^{\mathsf T})^\perp = N(A)R(AT)⊥=N(A). This chapter puts that fact to work. We’ll first repackage it as the Fundamental Theorem of Linear Algebra — a clean geometric picture of how AAA, viewed as a transformation, splits both its domain and codomain in two. From there we turn to a question that picture raises naturally: among all the possible bases for a subspace, is there a best one? The answer is yes — an orthonormal basis — and understanding why leads us through Fourier expansion, orthogonal matrices, and orthogonal projectors, the last of which is the essential tool behind the [[QR Factorization|QRQRQR factorization]] we’ll build in the next chapter.

Recap: Orthogonal Complements of the Fundamental Subspaces

Before building on last chapter’s result, it’s worth recalling why it’s true, since the same short argument will resurface throughout this chapter.

Orthogonal Complements (Recap)

For A∈Rm×nA \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}A∈Rm×n, R(A)⊥=N(AT)andR(AT)⊥=N(A).R(A)^\perp = N(A^{\mathsf T}) \qquad \text{and} \qquad R(A^{\mathsf T})^\perp = N(A).R(A)⊥=N(AT)andR(AT)⊥=N(A).

Sketch

Suppose x∈R(A)⊥x \in R(A)^\perpx∈R(A)⊥, i.e., xxx is orthogonal to every vector Ay∈R(A)Ay \in R(A)Ay∈R(A). Then for all y∈Rny \in \mathbb{R}^ny∈Rn,

0=⟨x,Ay⟩=xT(Ay)=(ATx)Ty=⟨ATx,y⟩.0 = \langle x, Ay\rangle = x^{\mathsf T}(Ay) = (A^{\mathsf T}x)^{\mathsf T}y = \langle A^{\mathsf T}x, y\rangle.0=⟨x,Ay⟩=xT(Ay)=(ATx)Ty=⟨ATx,y⟩.

An inner product that vanishes against every yyy forces ATx=0⃗A^{\mathsf T}x = \vec 0ATx=0, i.e., x∈N(AT)x \in N(A^{\mathsf T})x∈N(AT). This proves R(A)⊥=N(AT)R(A)^\perp = N(A^{\mathsf T})R(A)⊥=N(AT); applying the identical argument with AAA replaced by ATA^{\mathsf T}AT gives R(AT)⊥=N(A)R(A^{\mathsf T})^\perp = N(A)R(AT)⊥=N(A).

Linear Transformations and the Fundamental Theorem of Linear Algebra

Every matrix A∈Rm×nA \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}A∈Rm×n can be viewed not just as a grid of numbers, but as a linear transformation carrying vectors in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn to vectors in Rm\mathbb{R}^mRm:

x∈Rn→ A Ax∈Rm,rank⁡(A)=r≤min⁡(m,n).x \in \mathbb{R}^n \xrightarrow{\ A\ } Ax \in \mathbb{R}^m, \qquad \operatorname{rank}(A) = r \le \min(m,n).x∈Rn A ​Ax∈Rm,rank(A)=r≤min(m,n).

Viewed this way, the orthogonal complement result from the previous section says something quite striking: it partitions the domain and the codomain of AAA into orthogonal pieces, with the rank rrr controlling the size of every piece. This is the Fundamental Theorem of Linear Algebra.

The Fundamental Theorem of Linear Algebra

Recall that dim⁡N(A)=(number of columns of A)−rank⁡(A)\dim N(A) = (\text{number of columns of } A) - \operatorname{rank}(A)dimN(A)=(number of columns of A)−rank(A), the nullity.

In the domain, Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn: R(AT)⊥N(A)R(A^{\mathsf T}) \perp N(A)R(AT)⊥N(A)

  • dim⁡R(AT)=r\dim R(A^{\mathsf T}) = rdimR(AT)=r
  • dim⁡N(A)=n−r\dim N(A) = n - rdimN(A)=n−r
  • Direct sum: Rn=R(AT)⊕N(A)\mathbb{R}^n = R(A^{\mathsf T}) \oplus N(A)Rn=R(AT)⊕N(A)

In the codomain, Rm\mathbb{R}^mRm: R(A)⊥N(AT)R(A) \perp N(A^{\mathsf T})R(A)⊥N(AT)

  • dim⁡R(A)=r\dim R(A) = rdimR(A)=r
  • dim⁡N(AT)=m−r\dim N(A^{\mathsf T}) = m - rdimN(AT)=m−r
  • Direct sum: Rm=R(A)⊕N(AT)\mathbb{R}^m = R(A) \oplus N(A^{\mathsf T})Rm=R(A)⊕N(AT)

In short: every vector in the domain decomposes uniquely into a “row-space part” and a “null-space part,” and AAA carries the row-space part faithfully into R(A)R(A)R(A) while collapsing the null-space part to zero. This picture — two orthogonal splittings, one on each side of AAA — is the geometric skeleton underneath everything else in this course, including the SVD later on.

Orthonormal Bases and Fourier Expansion

The Fundamental Theorem tells us which subspaces matter. It says nothing yet about which basis to use for them. As it happens, one choice of basis makes every computation involving a subspace dramatically simpler.

“The best basis is an orthonormal basis.”

Orthonormal Basis

A set of vectors B={u1,u2,…,un}B = \{u_1, u_2, \dots, u_n\}B={u1​,u2​,…,un​} is an orthonormal basis for an inner product space if every vector is orthogonal to every other, and each has unit length:

⟨ui,uj⟩=uiTuj=δij,\langle u_i, u_j \rangle = u_i^{\mathsf T}u_j = \delta_{ij},⟨ui​,uj​⟩=uiT​uj​=δij​,

where δij\delta_{ij}δij​ is the Kronecker delta (111 if i=ji=ji=j, 000 otherwise).

The payoff of orthonormality is immediate: because the basis vectors are mutually independent and already scaled to length one, the coefficients needed to write any vector xxx in this basis can be read off directly, with no linear system to solve.

Generalized Fourier Expansion

For an orthonormal basis {u1,…,un}\{u_1,\dots,u_n\}{u1​,…,un​}, any vector xxx in the space decomposes as

x=⟨u1,x⟩u1+⟨u2,x⟩u2+⋯+⟨un,x⟩un.x = \langle u_1, x\rangle u_1 + \langle u_2, x\rangle u_2 + \cdots + \langle u_n, x\rangle u_n.x=⟨u1​,x⟩u1​+⟨u2​,x⟩u2​+⋯+⟨un​,x⟩un​.

Each scalar ⟨ui,x⟩\langle u_i, x\rangle⟨ui​,x⟩ is called a Fourier coefficient — geometrically, it is the length of xxx‘s projection onto the direction of uiu_iui​.

Orthogonal Matrices

Collecting an orthonormal basis into the columns of a matrix gives us a new, extremely well-behaved class of matrix.

Orthogonal Matrices

Place orthonormal vectors u1,…,unu_1,\dots,u_nu1​,…,un​ as the columns of a matrix

Q=[u1u2⋯un].Q = \begin{bmatrix} u_1 & u_2 & \cdots & u_n \end{bmatrix}.Q=[u1​​u2​​⋯​un​​].

Because the columns are orthonormal, QTQ=IQ^{\mathsf T}Q = IQTQ=I:

QTQ=[u1Tu2T⋮unT][u1u2⋯un]=I.Q^{\mathsf T}Q = \begin{bmatrix} u_1^{\mathsf T} \\ u_2^{\mathsf T} \\ \vdots \\ u_n^{\mathsf T} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u_1 & u_2 & \cdots & u_n \end{bmatrix} = I.QTQ=​u1T​u2T​⋮unT​​​[u1​​u2​​⋯​un​​]=I.

[!tip] Nomenclature Check

  • If QQQ is rectangular, we say it “has orthonormal columns.” Here QTQ=IQ^{\mathsf T}Q = IQTQ=I, but QQT≠IQQ^{\mathsf T} \ne IQQT=I in general.
  • If QQQ is square, we call it an orthogonal matrix. Here QT=Q−1Q^{\mathsf T} = Q^{-1}QT=Q−1, so QTQ=QQT=IQ^{\mathsf T}Q = QQ^{\mathsf T} = IQTQ=QQT=I.

Orthogonal matrices have a property that makes them especially trustworthy in numerical computation: they never distort length.

Preservation of Length

For an orthogonal matrix QQQ, ∥Qx∥=∥x∥\|Qx\| = \|x\|∥Qx∥=∥x∥ for every vector xxx.

Proof
∥Qx∥2=⟨Qx,Qx⟩=(Qx)T(Qx)=xTQTQx=xTIx=xTx=∥x∥2.■\|Qx\|^2 = \langle Qx, Qx\rangle = (Qx)^{\mathsf T}(Qx) = x^{\mathsf T}Q^{\mathsf T}Qx = x^{\mathsf T}Ix = x^{\mathsf T}x = \|x\|^2. \qquad \blacksquare∥Qx∥2=⟨Qx,Qx⟩=(Qx)T(Qx)=xTQTQx=xTIx=xTx=∥x∥2.■

Rotations, reflections, and permutation matrices are all familiar examples of orthogonal matrices — and all three, geometrically, preserve size while possibly changing direction.

Elementary Orthogonal Projectors

Orthonormal bases and orthogonal matrices both point toward the same underlying operation: projecting a vector onto a subspace. It’s worth isolating that operation on its own, starting with the simplest possible case — projecting onto a single direction.

Elementary Orthogonal Projector

The orthogonal projector PuP_uPu​ onto the direction of a vector uuu takes any xxx and flattens it onto the line spanned by uuu. If uuu is a unit vector (∥u∥=1\|u\|=1∥u∥=1), the projector is the outer product

Pu=uuT.P_u = uu^{\mathsf T}.Pu​=uuT.

If uuu is not already normalized, divide by its squared length:

Pu=uuTuTu.P_u = \frac{uu^{\mathsf T}}{u^{\mathsf T}u}.Pu​=uTuuuT​.

Applying this projector to a vector xxx reveals exactly why it deserves the name “projection” — the result is nothing more than the Fourier-coefficient formula from earlier, applied to a single basis vector.

Action of the Projector

Taking uuu to be a unit vector,

Pux=(uuT)x=u(uTx)=u⟨u,x⟩=⟨u,x⟩ u,P_u x = (uu^{\mathsf T})x = u(u^{\mathsf T}x) = u\langle u, x\rangle = \langle u, x\rangle\, u,Pu​x=(uuT)x=u(uTx)=u⟨u,x⟩=⟨u,x⟩u,

using associativity of matrix multiplication to regroup uTxu^{\mathsf T}xuTx as the scalar ⟨u,x⟩\langle u, x\rangle⟨u,x⟩. The result is a vector pointing in the direction of uuu, scaled by exactly the projection length of xxx onto uuu — precisely the single-term case of the Fourier expansion above.

The same idea extends immediately from a single direction to an entire subspace: if instead of one unit vector we have a whole matrix of orthonormal columns, the outer product uuTuu^{\mathsf T}uuT generalizes to QQTQQ^{\mathsf T}QQT.

Projector onto a Subspace Spanned by Orthonormal Columns

If Q∈Rm×kQ \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times k}Q∈Rm×k has orthonormal columns, the orthogonal projector onto R(Q)R(Q)R(Q) is

P=QQT,P = QQ^{\mathsf T},P=QQT,

which satisfies PT=PP^{\mathsf T} = PPT=P and P2=PP^2 = PP2=P. Moreover, for any xxx, QQTxQQ^{\mathsf T}xQQTx is the unique vector in R(Q)R(Q)R(Q) closest to xxx in Euclidean norm.

This lemma is more than a generalization for its own sake: it is the exact mechanism the QRQRQR factorization relies on. Building QQQ one orthonormal column at a time — via Gram–Schmidt — amounts to repeatedly projecting a new vector onto the space already spanned, and subtracting that projection away. That construction is where we turn next.
